what_it_does: Documents the local Ollama runtime setup, the procedure for registering Hugging Face cache GGUF files via Modelfiles, the available API endpoints, the inventory of cloud-routed models, and operational lifecycle notes.
observations: Ollama runs as a system systemd unit as the ollama user, listening on localhost:11434 with both native /api/* and OpenAI-compatible /v1/* endpoints.
Three local models are registered (two Qwen3.6 GGUFs and nomic-embed-text) with sizes; ten cloud routes are listed that proxy to Ollama Cloud and incur per-token cost.
Registering a GGUF normally copies it into ~/.ollama/models/, but pointing the Modelfile at the HF-cache path can avoid duplication, though symlink behavior is not fully characterized.
Modelfile directives covered are FROM, TEMPLATE, SYSTEM, and PARAMETER for temperature, top_p, num_ctx, and num_gpu.
Cloud routes look like local models to API clients but call Ollama Cloud; they can be removed with ollama rm.
The service is system-managed, survives WSL reboots without user linger, queues concurrent requests, and vLLM is recommended for high-throughput batched serving.

An 'Open at this topic' section lists unresolved gaps: chat-template override pattern, tool-calling reliability, cloud cost/latency/rate limits, disk-usage growth, pending model registrations, and Open WebUI integration.
standouts: Cloud routes expose remote models as local API tags with an explicit cost warning.
The no-copy registration trick attempts to avoid doubling disk usage by referencing the HF-cache path directly.
The document distinguishes Ollama's system-level systemd service from user-level vLLM services.
conventions: Uses frontmatter with type, title, date_started, and tags.
Uses wiki-style links to operations index, runtimes, and individual model files.
Organizes content into State, registration steps, endpoint tables, lifecycle, open questions, and connections.
Explicitly flags known unknowns and pending tasks rather than hiding them.
concerns: Cloud-route cost, latency, and rate-limit behavior are undocumented and could lead to unexpected spend.
No-copy registration relies on Ollama following HF-cache symlinks correctly, which is not fully verified.
Tool-calling reliability for the registered Qwen3.6 variants is untested.
Some downloaded models are not yet registered, so the substrate state is incomplete.
notes: This is the operational runbook for the local Ollama substrate, covering model registration, API surface, cloud routes, and known gaps.
questions: What is the exact disk-usage behavior when Ollama follows HF-cache symlinks during no-copy registration?
How are cloud routes authenticated and billed?
What is the precise Ollama TEMPLATE syntax to override the chat template for Qwen3.5/3.6 variants?
Does Ollama reliably support tool calling for the registered Qwen3.6 models?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose (registration, Modelfile, cloud routes) matches the content; it functions as a runtime operations guide.
apparent_themes: Local-first model serving augmented by optional cloud capacity.
Operational pragmatism around disk usage, service scope, and endpoint compatibility.
Explicit documentation of unfinished integration points and unknowns.
intention_signals: To make the local Ollama runtime discoverable, reproducible, and safely integrated with both local GGUF models and cloud-hosted capacity while clearly flagging costs and unfinished integration points.
novelty: The detailed no-copy GGUF registration pattern and the explicit catalog of cloud-routed models with cost warnings are distinctive operational notes.
contribution: A concrete, file-local operational reference for Ollama: runtime state, registration steps, endpoint mapping, cloud-route inventory, lifecycle commands, and a checklist of open integration questions.
summary_for_embedding: An operations document describing the local Ollama runtime, registered local and cloud-routed models, GGUF registration via Modelfiles, API endpoints, and service lifecycle.
intention_for_embedding: To record how to operate and extend the Ollama model-serving substrate, including local registrations and cloud-route usage.
